query: amino acids involved in collagen synthesis

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Amino acids are the building blocks of proteins; therefore, it is no surprise that collagen is comprised of amino [4] acids. The primary amino acid sequence of collagen is glycine-proline-X or glycine-X-hydroxyproline [5] X can be any of the other 17 amino acids, and every third amino acid is glycine.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Collagen is protein molecules made up of amino acids. It provides structural support to the extracellular space of connective tissues. Due to its rigidity and resistance to stretching, it is the perfect matrix for skin, tendons, bones, and ligaments.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Connective Tissue -- Cellular Level -- Collagen Synthesis and Structure
retrieved_docs.content: The synthesis of collagen begins with DNA translation of a polypeptide chain in the rough endoplasmic reticulum of intracellular space (See Figure. Review of Collagen Synthesis). This polypeptide chain is called preprocollagen, composed of alpha chains with repeating amino acid glycine-X-Y sequences (X and Y are proline or lysine). [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Collagen is composed of 3 chains. The chains are wound together to form a triple helix. Since glycine is the smallest of all the amino acids, it allows the chain to form a tight configuration, and and it can withstand stress.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Amino Acid Synthesis and Degradation -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Glycine is not considered essential to the human diet. The body can synthesize this amino acid from the amino acid serine. However, the metabolic capacity for glycine biosynthesis does not satisfy the need for collagen synthesis in several organisms. In some of the vertebrate livers, glycine synthesis is catalyzed by glycine synthase, also known as glycine cleavage enzyme. Glycine is integral to the creation of alpha-helices in secondary protein structure, and, mainly, it is the most copious amino acid in collagen harboring triple-helices. Glycine is also an inhibitory neurotransmitter. The interference of its release within the central nervous system (spinal cord) can induce spastic paralysis due to uninhibited muscle contraction. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Clinical Significance -- Scurvy
retrieved_docs.content: This is a family of genetic disorders that affect the bones making them weak and easily breakable. Inheritance is autosomal dominant, and most cases are due to mutations in the COL1A1 or COL1A2 genes. There are 8 types, each with differing degrees of severity with type 1 the mildest and type II the most severe. [7] Gene mutations affect procollagen formation in which the small glycine amino acid is substituted for bulkier amino acids which alter the collagen triple helix structure.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Connective Tissue -- Cellular Level -- Collagen Synthesis and Structure
retrieved_docs.content: The proline and lysine residues of the alpha chain become hydroxylated with the help of vitamin C-dependent hydroxylases; hydroxylation helps form a stable structure. Vitamin C deficiency results in impaired hydroxylation of pre-procollagen and defective collagen synthesis, a condition known as scurvy. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: The process of collagen synthesis occurs mainly in the cells of fibroblasts which are specialized cells with the main function of synthesizing collagen and stroma. Collagen synthesis occurs both intracellularly and extracellularly. Although different types of collagen may undergo different post-translational modifications, the basic outline for collagen synthesis is listed below.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Cellular Level -- Post-translational modification
retrieved_docs.content: Once in the ER, the pre-pro-polypeptide undergoes post-translational processing where three major modifications are made to the pre-pro-polypeptide for it to become pro-collagen. The signal peptide on the N-terminal is removed The lysine and proline residues get additional hydroxyl groups added to them via hydroxylase enzymes which require vitamin C as a cofactor Glycosylation of the selected hydroxyl groups on lysine with galactose and glucose b Three of the hydroxylated and glycosylated pro-a-chains assemble by twisting into a triple helix by zipper-like folding. The triple helix configuration is 3 left-handed helices twisted into a right-handed coil Now the pro-collagen molecule is ready to move to the Golgi apparatus for final modifications and assembled into secretory vesicles to enter the extracellular space

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Connective Tissue -- Cellular Level -- Collagen Synthesis and Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Next, the hydroxylated lysine of the alpha chain undergoes glycosylation with the addition of a carbohydrate. After glycosylation, the formation of disulfide bonds and hydrogen bonds between 3 different alpha chains forms a triple helix. The resultant structure is now procollagen. Defective triple helix formation leads to osteogenesis imperfecta and impaired bone matrix synthesis. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Cellular Level -- Collagen Fibril Assembly
retrieved_docs.content: Lysyl oxidase a copper-dependent enzyme acts on lysine and hydroxylysines, and covalent bonding between tropocollagen molecules form a collagen fibril

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: As with any biochemical pathway, there are numerous steps that are carefully executed and are tightly regulate and controlled. However, with many steps in processing collagen, there can be genetic mutations which can lead to errors in assembly, posttranslational modification or nutritional deficiencies which can affect enzymatic function. Some examples include:

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Connective Tissue -- Cellular Level -- Collagen Synthesis and Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Next, tropocollagen is reinforced with many adjacent tropocollagen molecules by covalent cross-linking of hydroxylated lysine residues, resulting in collagen fibrils. The cross-linking of hydroxylysine residues is made possible through a copper-dependent lysyl oxidase enzyme. Finally, several collagen fibrils accumulate to form a thick bundle; the end product is collagen fibers. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Osteogenesis Imperfecta -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Two pro-alpha-1 chains and one pro-alpha-2 chain make up type I collagen, which forms the main protein of the extracellular membrane of skin, bones, tendons, etc., and create a rigid, triple helix structure. Each alpha chain consists of an amino-terminal pro-peptide, carboxyl-terminal pro-peptide, and a central pro-peptide consisting of 338 glycine repeats. Glycine is the smallest residue that can occupy the axial position of the triple helix. [12] The triple helix structure of type I collagen is possible because of the presence of glycine at every third amino acid residue.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Collagen is the most abundant protein in the human body. Therefore, it can be divided into many types. The most common types of collagen are types I through V each serving different functions.

Across all these products, lysine is consistently described using the relationships “has_active_ingredient,” “has_active_moiety,” or “has_ingredient,” indicating that it is an essential and deliberately included amino acid in parenteral nutrition formulations. Its presence alongside other essential amino acids and, in some cases, glucose, electrolytes, and stabilizing agents underscores lysine’s critical role in supporting protein synthesis, tissue repair, and overall metabolic needs of patients who require intravenous nutritional support.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 427
retrieved_docs.content: Amino Acids, I.V. solution additive is a formulation that incorporates alanylglutamine, a dipeptide derived from the dipeptidy structure. This intravenous additive is also linked to the class of Antifibrinolytic amino acids, a broader group that includes specific agents referred to as antifibrinolytika. Together, these components highlight the role of alanylglutamine as a peptide‑based ingredient within IV amino‑acid solutions and the relevance of antifibrinolytic amino acids, including antifibrinolytika, for their potential to inhibit fibrinolysis in clinical settings.

retrieved_docs.content: Collectively, the knowledge base underscores lysine’s dual role as a pharmacologically active amino acid in parenteral nutrition and antifibrinolytic preparations, and as a non‑active excipient in diverse dermatologic and cosmetic products, while also highlighting the broader utilization of comprehensive amino‑acid mixtures—including tryptophan—in both systemic and topical therapeutic contexts.

The concurrent use of tryptophan as an inactive ingredient further illustrates the broader application of essential amino acids beyond their nutritional functions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 176
retrieved_docs.content: Amino Acids, I.V. solution additive is classified as an i.v. solution additive and includes alanylglutamine, which is a dipeptide and a member of this amino‑acid additive group. This additive category is linked to Antifibrinolytic amino acids, a broader class that encompasses antifibrinolytika, indicating a role in inhibiting fibrinolysis. Other i.v. solution additives in the ATC system and Vitamin IV solution additives are also subtypes of i.v. solution additives, with the latter specifically associated with vitamin D. Vitamin D appears in several formulations: it is an ingredient of multivitamin tablets and is related to IRON/MULTIVITAMS LIQUID, reflecting its inclusion in multivitamin and iron‑containing liquid preparations. Within the vitamin‑IV additive context, vitamin D is connected to inhibitory bone resorption (inhibitory kostní resorpce), underscoring its function in reducing bone turnover. Moreover, vitamin D is related to osteoporosis (هشاشة العظام), highlighting its importance in bone health and the prevention of bone‑density loss. Together, these relationships illustrate how amino‑acid and vitamin‑based intravenous additives are organized, their biochemical roles (e.g., dipeptide composition, antifibrinolytic activity), and the central role of vitamin D in supporting bone metabolism and mitigating osteoporosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 47
retrieved_docs.content: Collectively, the data illustrate lysine’s central role as an essential nutrient in parenteral amino‑acid solutions and its ancillary function as an excipient in dermatologic and cosmetic products, while also highlighting its utility in advanced drug‑design strategies. This comprehensive mapping aids healthcare professionals in optimizing nutritional support, anticipating formulation‑related effects, and exploiting lysine‑based conjugates for targeted therapies.

These additives allow precise customization of amino‑acid concentrations, electrolyte balance, preservative content (sulfite‑free versus sulfite‑containing), and caloric provision to meet the diverse metabolic and hepatic needs of patients receiving intravenous feeding. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2001
retrieved_docs.content: Amino Acids, I.V. solution additive are linked to Antifibrinolytic amino acids, indicating that certain intravenous amino‑acid formulations possess antifibrinolytic activity. Antifibrinolytic amino acids are also referred to by the term antifibrinolytika, reflecting their classification as agents that inhibit fibrinolysis. This relationship highlights the therapeutic relevance of using amino‑acid IV additives to provide antifibrinolytic effects in clinical practice.
